Actually, I think there is a rather ugly issue with re-issuing new tokens: Imagine you have two separate instances of an application using the same appid/secret. You also only keep one token per user/application combo to avoid storing a ton of tokens (especially if you use a very high expiry).

Now whenever you authorize using application 1, application 2 will need a new token and vice versa. This is extremely ugly, especially when users use oauth to login but your application also uses the tokens to perform background tasks (which they now can't do anymore because the login to the other application just invalidated its own token).
I am closing the issue then.

That's pretty much the intended behaviour because the `AnimatedMarker()` doesn't adjust to the width of the bar (which is the default for static markers).

Static markers use the following code to automatically adjust to width:
https://github.com/WoLpH/python-progressbar/blob/32d5217db02430a2c3373e3e6f4fc5683f5efa1f/progressbar/widgets.py#L34-L48

By inheriting the `AnimatedMarker` you can easily change the behaviour so it works like a normal marker:
```python
class GrowingAnimatedMarker(AnimatedMarker):

    def __call__(self, progress, data, width=None):
        marker = AnimatedMarker.__call__(self, progress, data, width)
        return marker * length
```

But the actual goal of the animated marker is to use it with bars of unknown width in conjunction with the bouncing bar:
```python
import time
import progressbar

test_size = 30

widgets = [progressbar.BouncingBar(marker=progressbar.AnimatedMarker())]
pbar = progressbar.ProgressBar(widgets=widgets).start()

for i in range(test_size):
    time.sleep(0.1)
    pbar.update(i)

pbar.finish()
```

Having the animated bar actually fill the screen looks a bit strange :)

It seems like that endpoint itself has changed or gone away (temporarily? permanently? can't say), which is the unfortunate nature of this system since it has no official API anymore.

I helped someone on StackOverflow with a 'workaround' of basically parsing the full response to get information.

https://stackoverflow.com/a/46081537/297696

Code from answer below:

```python
import json
import requests

rsp = requests.get('https://finance.google.com/finance?q=AAPL&output=json')
if rsp.status_code in (200,):

    # This magic here is to cut out various leading characters from the JSON 
    # response, as well as trailing stuff (a terminating ']\n' sequence), and then
    # we decode the escape sequences in the response
    # This then allows you to load the resulting string
    # with the JSON module.
    fin_data = json.loads(rsp.content[6:-2].decode('unicode_escape'))

    # print out some quote data
    print('Opening Price: {}'.format(fin_data['op']))
    print('Price/Earnings Ratio: {}'.format(fin_data['pe']))
    print('52-week high: {}'.format(fin_data['hi52']))
    print('52-week low: {}'.format(fin_data['lo52']))
```

A derivative of this could integrated into the library assuming the other endpoint doesn't come back (and a better endpoint isn't found).

I really like python-redmine. It was a big part of our choice to user Redmine overall. Thanks for your work so far on it! In the spirit of improvement, here are two related thoughts to this issue.

+1 to not bundling certificates into `python-redmine`. Recommend that people use `certifi` (as you did here). It's going to be more up to date and trust decisions should be an explicit user decision for security reasons.

+1 to not bundling `python-requests` with `python-redmine` at all. I can see why it would be helpful/easy given the difficulty with different versions users will run, but consider instead picking a minimum version of `python-requests` and letting easy_install or pip install the *real* `python-requests`. This may cause friction for some users, but they can choose to upgrade or not. As the developer, you'll focus on making your software integrate well with `python-requests` instead of repackaging `python-requests` inside of `python-redmine`. Also you'll get compatibility testing of newer versions of `python-requests` as users receive newer and newer versions and they can always downgrade to workaround the incompatibility until a newer `python-redmine` can be released that is compatible with the latest `python-requests`. This will make it easier for `python-redmine` to switch newer versions of `python-requests` which eventually it will need to do.

Here's a security example relevant also motivating the two above points.

(1) Consider that a CA was deemed insecure by Mozilla and/or `certifi` and revoked. If it was carried inside of `python-redmine` you'll be distributing insecure trust info until you update `python-redmine` and have your users upgrade to the new `python-redmine`.

(2) What if a security vulnerability is discovered in `python-requests` 2.8.1? A new version will be released with the fix, but instead of upgrading just `certifi` and feeling safe, we also would have to upgrade `python-redmine` assuming a "new" version has been released.

In both cases additional, unnecessary work had to be done and it's still vulnerable for longer. Thanks again for the very excellent `python-redmine`!

From what I can tell, the problem stems from flask-restplus trying to implement it's own error handlers in an `api` decorated class, and any exceptions raised in that class get caught by their error handlers and never make it back to the native flask errorhandlers, which is what this extension users. This will be a problem with every error that this extension returns, they will all be eaten by flask-restplus, not just the ones you are trying to override.

I'm not sure why they went that route, but there isn't much I can do about it in my extension. Breaking native flask functionality does not make for an extension that plays nice with others. I pinged them about this issue, and if they get back to me with how it could be resolved I'll make sure to update this. In the mean time, the only things I could think to do would be to either:
* dump flask-restplus (personally for my REST apis, I use either swagger with connexion or normal flask routes with marshmallow for request parsing)
* extend flask-restplus by subclassing the Api object and making sure uncaught exceptions make it back to the flask errorhandlers, or
* try do some magic to convert all registered flask errorhanders into Api errorhandlers at runtime.

Hello, thanks for reporting here.

M2M fields are not supported as translatable fields, because it is inherently ambiguous. From your model, it seems this is not needed anyway, as Degree should not be different depending on display language.

More generally speaking, you cannot just dump all of your fields as translated fields, you must split between those for which it makes sense, and those for which it does not.

In some edge cases such as logging translation changes, m2m translated fields might be marginally useful. In that case, refer to https://github.com/KristianOellegaard/django-hvad/issues/284#issuecomment-196777422 for solutions.

It's because the `OpenAPI Renderer` is using `CoreAPI Renderer` to supply `Swagger` with schema.
The problem is, `CoreAPI` does not support nested fields definition.

However, DRF already provides a full schema as metadata.
http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/metadata/

It seem to also be used internally for the Browseable API.
The way `OpenAPI` is generated should use metadata instead relying on the `CoreAPI Renderer`.

My use case would be to identify n-grams as part of a language
identification toolkit (for a university assignment).

I think you should have 3 sets of functions - keep CJK as it is (just
hanzi/kanji/hanja) characters only, and then create two new ones: is_hangul
and is_hiragana/is_katakana (I don't know how it should be named in order
to take into account of both hiragana and katakana sets).

I'm using n-gram analysis, and in the case of Asian languages, n-grams just
doesn't exist because for the most part, each character itself is already a
word. So I'd like it to be such that if it detects a block of text to have
any Han, hangul, hiragana or katakana characters (classify them all as
Oriental characters just to make it easy), it just marks it as having such
characters. That'd make things easier for me so that if a block of text has
these stuff (assume that it's cleaned by myself prior to running the
character identification functions), then it sets n=1 for n-grams.

What you're seeing is the Unicode character U+2588 FULL BLOCK and similar block drawing characters, originally from cp437, the ASCII extension used in DOS. Your terminal font is not drawing them at full height. This was made the default for the "qr" script because it allows more data to fit on screen -- the older approach requires each "pixel" to be two characters wide to get something vaguely square. For what it's worth, my phone is able to scan all three of your screenshots fine.

#mx.symbol_doc.SymbolDoc.get_output_shape()
def N_gamma(seq_len,batch_size,vocab_size,embed_dim,hidden_num,N):    
    data = mx.sym.Variable('data')
    embed_weight=mx.sym.Variable('embed_weight')
    H_weight=mx.sym.Variable('H_weight')
    d_bias=mx.sym.Variable('d_bias')
    U_weight=mx.sym.Variable('U_weight')
    W_weight=mx.sym.Variable('W_weight')
    b_bias=mx.sym.Variable('b_bias')
    b0_bias=mx.sym.Variable('b0_bias')
    label = mx.sym.Variable('softmax_label')
    embed = mx.sym.Embedding(data=data, input_dim=vocab_size, weight=embed_weight,output_dim=embed_dim, name='embed')
    wordvec = mx.sym.SliceChannel(data=embed,num_outputs=seq_len+N-1,squeeze_axis=1)
    y_all=[]
    for seq_idx in range(N-1,seq_len+N-1):
        word=[wordvec[i] for i in xrange(seq_idx-N+1,seq_idx+1)]
        hidden_concat = mx.sym.Concat(*word, dim=1)
        tanh_hidden=mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=hidden_concat,num_hidden=hidden_num,weight=H_weight,bias=d_bias,name='tanh_hidden')
        tanh_act=mx.sym.Activation(data=tanh_hidden, act_type="tanh")
        tanh_part=mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=tanh_act,num_hidden=vocab_size,weight=U_weight,bias=b_bias,name='tanh_part')
        fc_part=mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=hidden_concat,num_hidden=vocab_size,weight=W_weight,bias=b0_bias,name='fc_part')
        y=tanh_part+fc_part
        y_all.append(y)
    y_concat=mx.sym.Concat(*y_all,dim=0)
    label = mx.sym.transpose(data=label)
    label = mx.sym.Reshape(data=label, target_shape=(0,))
    sm = mx.sym.SoftmaxOutput(data=y_concat, label=label, name='softmax')
    return sm`

The paper you linked actually says that stride should be applied on the first conv in a block (1x1) - "Downsampling is performed by conv3_1, conv4_1, and conv5_1 with a stride of 2" (description of Table 1, you can also look at the original authors' caffe prototxt). The gluon version follows Torch/pyTorch and is "wrong", in that it does not follow the paper (and there is a PR to fix it #8414). Accuracy achieved from both versions is basically the same, the version from the paper has ~10% faster execution though.

import mxnet as mx
import numpy as np
from n_gamma import N_gamma
from word2vec_io import BucketSentenceIter
import pickle as pk

def Perplexity(label, pred):
    label = label.T.reshape((-1,))
    loss = 0.
    for i in range(pred.shape[0]):
        loss += -np.log(max(1e-10, pred[i][int(label[i])]))
    return np.exp(loss / label.size)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    batch_size=64
    hidden_num = 128
    embed_dim = 128
    N=5
    vocab=pk.load(open('.//data//dict_news_lstm20160804.pkl','r'))
    vocab_size=len(vocab)
    data=pk.load(open('.//data//train.pkl','r'))
    data=[i[0] for i in data]
    data_val=pk.load(open('.//data//test.pkl','r'))
    data_val=[i[0] for i in data_val]
    def sym_gen(seq_len):
        return N_gamma(seq_len,batch_size,vocab_size,embed_dim,hidden_num,N)
    train=BucketSentenceIter(data,batch_size,N)
    test=BucketSentenceIter(data_val,batch_size,N)
    symbol=sym_gen
    model = mx.model.FeedForward(ctx=[mx.context.gpu(i) for i in range(1)],
                                 symbol=symbol,
                                 num_epoch=10,
                                 learning_rate=0.01,
                                 momentum=0.0,
                                 wd=0.00001,
                                 initializer=mx.init.Xavier(factor_type="in", magnitude=2.34))

    import logging
    #head = '%(asctime)-15s %(message)s'
    #logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG, format=head)
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG,
                    filename='./train.log',
                    filemode='w')
    console = logging.StreamHandler()
    console.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
    logging.getLogger().addHandler(console)

    model.fit(X=train, eval_data=test,
              eval_metric = mx.metric.np(Perplexity),
			  epoch_end_callback=mx.callback.do_checkpoint("./output/5_gram"),
              batch_end_callback=mx.callback.Speedometer(batch_size, 100),)``

From what you described, this is more like an issue of the graph executor assigning wrong `req` to the input of the `transpose` operator.
```python
import mxnet as mx


def ttake(x, i):
    """Take from axis 1 instead of 0."""
    return mx.sym.flatten(mx.sym.transpose(mx.sym.take(mx.sym.transpose(x), i)))


shape = (4, 5)
data = mx.sym.Variable('data', shape=(4, 5))
indices = mx.sym.Variable('indices', shape=(1,))
sym = ttake(data, indices)
exe = sym.simple_bind(ctx=mx.cpu(), grad_req={'data': 'write', 'indices': 'null'})
exe.arg_dict[data.name][:] = mx.nd.arange(shape[0] * shape[1]).reshape(shape)
exe.arg_dict[indices.name][:] = mx.nd.array([0], dtype='int32')
print(exe.arg_dict[data.name])
output = exe.forward()[0]
exe.backward(output)
print(exe.grad_arrays[0].asnumpy())
```

Initially, my thinking was that stack() would build a whole stack frame
when we're only interested in the second-to-last frame, and that this was
the source of the slowness (especially in the context of a web request,
where the stack might be quite deep).  However, it seems though that
_get_frame is much cheaper even with a very short stack:

import inspect

import sys


def f():

    fileloc = inspect.getsourcefile(inspect.stack()[1][0])   # This
version: 1.66 seconds


#    fileloc = sys._getframe().f_back.f_code.co_filename     # This
version: 0.06 seconds



for n in range(10000):

    f()

I don't have an explanation for that and I haven't found any documentation
for it; it's just an empirical observation for now.  As to whether this
could be more brittle - it's a great question - I'm not sure at this

@dhruvsinghal updated the pull request.

Using `subscribe` to detect a `watch-del` works well, but I've found a minor annoyance. If a project directory is deleted from the filesystem, the 'subscription' event has no property to signal that.

Two possible solutions:
- include `deleted: true` in the cancel event
- send a separate 'subscription' event like `{name: '/', ...}` before the cancel event
- make the user create a second subscription that watches the project directory explicitly if they want to know about its deletion

The first solution has the potential to be confusing, since someone may think `deleted` means the subscription was canceled by a `watch-del` command.

The third solution seems a little heavy-handed / inconvenient.

The second solution seems best, but I admit `name: '/'` is strange since other 'subscription' events don't include a leading backslash.

Not only is it a pain to chase around the changing library name, but it is very difficult to specify this library name via distutils.

When underlying TCP connections are lost, zmq will automatically reconnect. That's what you are seeing. It's happening in this test case after you have requested that both sockets be closed because of the zmq LINGER option. Since you close the receiver before you close the client, there are undelivered messages waiting in the client's queue to be delivered when a server reappears. Calling 'close' on a zmq socket means: request that the underlying socket be closed once its pending messages have finished processing. It is an async request, not the actual closure of the underlying socket. LINGER provides a timeout for how long to wait on undelivered messages before discarding them. The default is -1, or forever. `ctx.term()` will block until all undelivered messages are delivered or discarded.

If discarding undelivered messages is okay for you, then you can either set linger when you create sockets:

```python
socket.linger = 1000 # 1000 milliseconds
```

or set linger when you close:

```python
socket.close(linger=3000) # give undelivered messages 3 seconds before discarding them
```

Either setting LINGER to a finite value or ensuring that all message are delivered will result in then underlying sockets being closed permanently.

If you can run through an entire epoch, then you know the batch size is less than or equal to its maximum. Finding the maximum is a process of,say, binary search, e.g. 20 gives out of memory error, 10 completes the epoch, 15 gives out of memory error, 12 completes the epoch, 13 gives out of memory error; so the maximum batch size is 12.

It considers the ordinal to be an unsigned 16-bit integer (range 0-65535) and therefore the ordinal number supplied it out of range. See spec for [`io.BytesIO`]( https://docs.python.org/2/library/io.html#io.BytesIO ). Also compare to [`StringIO.StringIO`]( https://docs.python.org/2/library/stringio.html#StringIO.StringIO ), which has a similar API. Neither have a `getbuffer` method. Only in Python 3.2 does [`io.BytesIO`]( https://docs.python.org/3.2/library/io.html#io.BytesIO ) get the `getbuffer` method. Note that it still has `getvalue` in the most recent iteration of [`io.BytesIO`]( https://docs.python.org/3/library/io.html#io.BytesIO ) in Python 3. In short, `getvalue` must be used to maintain Python 2 and Python 3 compatibility.

I believe I have identified the issue and solution to this issue. It looks like it's the urllib3 lib on GAE that's the issue here. Details can be found [here](http://urllib3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/advanced-usage.html#google-app-engine). In short, add the following to your yaml.

env_variables:
    GAE_USE_SOCKETS_HTTPLIB : 'true'

import boto3

print("Put object...")

              Key='encrypt-key', Body=b'foobar',
              SSECustomerKey=KEY,
              SSECustomerAlgorithm='AES256')
print("Done")
# Make sure to save the KEY!

                         Key='encrypt-key',

```

This is likely the behavior from the service end. The reason why it appears fine in the CLI is that it automatically paginates for you. import boto3

p = boto3.client('ssm').get_paginator('describe_parameters')

I'm over three years late to this thread but there is (now?!) another way to do this!

Use a deferred that conditionally returns `colander.required`:

```python
@colander.deferred
def conditionally_required(node, kw):
    some_condition = # whatever conditional logic you want
    if some_condition:
        return colander.drop # won't be required (or return a default like `None`)
    return colander.required # will be required
```

This can then be used as the `missing` value when defining a schema node:

```python
foo = colander.SchemaNode(
    colander.String(),
    missing=conditionally_required
)
```

As a concrete example, if you have the request bound to your schema as `kw['request']` and you wanted to make `foo` required unless a field called `action` has the value `cheat`, you could use:

```python
@colander.deferred
def required_unless_cheating(node, kw):
    request = kw['request']
    action = request.params.get('action')
    if action == 'cheat':
        return colander.drop
    return colander.required    
```

@willhug `run_sync` won't work, unfortunately. What `run_sync` does is:
1. starts the IOLoop
1. runs the function
1. stops the IOLoop

Unfortunately, if you have an already running IOLoop step 1 throws an exception. So any tornado-based application would break. Actually, reading through @breerly's implementation of [threadloop](https://github.com/breerly/threadloop) (which tchannel uses for its "sync" implementation), it would break in non-tornado apps too as that just runs an IOLoop on a separate thread.

What we probably want to do is use `IOLoop.add_callback`, which will call the method on the next loop.

Putting this on hold. We may be able to solve the problem without exposing this API.

A wrapper like that would address the infinite loop problem, but it doesn't address the problem of the robot not being able to do something else while the loop is happening.

Since we're in python, technically you could wrap something like this with a generator, and that would address the problem of infinite looping and parallel execution -- though, as James says, it would break if you forgot to use the magic voodoo.

I've contemplated use of generators in RobotPy related stuff, but haven't really done anything there yet. The [yeti](http://yeti-robotics-runtime.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) framework uses asyncio + generator based coroutines, but I think there's still a bit of refinement needed before I would recommend something like to novices.

I like the idea of using TOML. I've been playing around with Rust a lot lately and I find that it's package management is really quite pleasant. 

I think that the pipfile should be 100% declarative and be in a pre-existing format. I think allowing it to be executable will lead to a slippery slope of dirty hacks in order to get unsupported use cases to work. I think creating a new syntax, whether it be Python inspired or not, reduces readability and increases cognitive load on the developer.

In my personal experience, I find that the standard `requirements.txt` approach works just fine. I think taking the approach of improving the current work flow is a good idea. Maybe just make `requirements.txt` more flexible and require it to be written in a common format(like TOML). It would be nice to be able to easily separate dev dependencies from deployment/release dependencies. Perhaps allow for per-environment and per-language version dependencies.

Something along the lines of:

```toml
# ~/requirements.TOML

[meta]
name = "my-application"
authors = ["John Doe <jdoe@email.com", "Jane Doe <janed@email.com>"]
license = "BSD2"
version = "1.1.0"

[default]
py-version = ">=3.4"

    [production]
    django = ">=1.9"

    requests = "latest"
    django-debug-toolbar = "latest"

[legacy]
py-version = "<=2.7"

```

I would suggest considering the _element of least surprise_. What format would be least surprising both for developers and IDEs/tooling?

I have good memory of how things feel in Ruby (bundler), Node (yarn, npm), Go, Rust's cargo, Java (ant, maven, gradle), Scala's sbt, .NET's nuget, Clojure and C++'s cmake.

I can tell you that you now control a very powerful knob. You can turn it inwards towards the Python community, or outwards towards the general developer population. An extreme example of keeping people out is Scala's SBT. A good example of inviting people in would be bundler (Ruby) and yarn/npm (Node).

If you want to welcome people in (and personally I think there's a ton of weight on a package manager for that), use a boring, omnipresent format. Use json, or yaml. If you want to keep people out, use Python or any new DSL which can be easy to build with a simple peg parser.

Hopefully this supports advancing a decision. Personally I am considering a Python comeback after more than 10 years, and I was amazingly surprised packaging is so painfully unsolved, still.

I'm not so sure. Fail fast is often a good strategy. What if one of the packages skipped is important - then it just pushes the error down the road. At least now the error is reported where the error occurs. It might be worthwhile suppressing the error and replacing it with a warning. The question is - which errors should we replace? Just access errors?

Also, I worry that by suppressing the error we're masking a more important problem - that the permissions defined in a package's tarball are applied to the installer's system. Hi, @engelkek,

The API gives you a way to choose a log filename.
This is done with the `LOG_FILE` setting when you schedule a spider run.
Any settings and spider arguments are lost after the spider is popped from the queue.

Currently it is only possible to retrieve the log files
of spiders that didn't get their log filenames overridden.
You can use the website directly for this,
it's just a GET request.

Currently, the documentation doesn't promise anything about it
but we don't plan to change the url path.
Even if we do, it will go through a deprecation circle with a warning.

It does make sense to be able to retrieve the log files even when the setting gets overridden
but this means that we'll have to start storing such settings even after the spider starts.
This needs some redesign. We are open for ideas.

@mrmachine I would like to see the AppConfig solution if you can share it.

If it is possible, it would be convenient to have @mrmachine's mixin added to an alternative AppConfig in `fluent_pages.apps` itself i.e.:

    class FluentAppCollectorPatchConfig(CollectorPatchMixin, FluentAppConfig):
        pass

Then users can specify the monkey-patching AppConfig in the `INSTALLED_APPS` settings, i.e. instead of `"fluent_pages"` they would put `"fluent_pages.apps.FluentAppCollectorPatchConfig"`.

Perhaps for those that don't want to use the monkey patch the default `FluentAppConfig` can provide a ready method that sets up the `pre_delete` solution (the collector patch mixin will override the ready method)? It is possible to find all FK fields on a model, and then look at the `on_delete` behavior on those field and perform that action (i.e. no need for per-model-settings or a default behavior). We could cache the `on_delete` behaviors per model class per field at startup for better performance.

class IAvailableAreas(zope.interface.Interface):

With the change here, a synchronizer that comes to the party late will have ``newTransaction`` called, even if ``begin`` hasn't been called. Why not just call ``newTransaction`` when we create a new transaction in either ``begin`` or ``get``?

I just want to add to @asanakoy's answer that deepdish is fully compatible with pandas too, essentially calling `to_hdf` internally. These two will create the same HDF5 file (both can be loaded with deepdish):

```
df.to_hdf(filename, key='table')
dd.io.save(filename, dict(table=df)
```
However, deepdish extends this by allowing you to save multiple data frames inside a single HDF5 file, intermixed with the other data types supported by deepdish:
```
dd.io.save(filename, dict(table=df, more_tables=[df1, df2, df3]))
```

I would to tell you more about this "delegation" concept.

I don't think most DataReader should have Pandas as dependency.
A DataReader should be able to perform a request and get a result.

If Pandas is installed and user want to use it we should import it and use it
if pandas is not installed or user don't want to use it we should return "raw" Python data (string, list, dict, ...)

Here is how I think delegation could work:

    # yahoo_daily.py

    import click

    import requests

    from pandas.compat import StringIO
    from pandas_datareader.utils import _sanitize_dates
    from delegate import Delegator

    _URL = 'http://ichart.finance.yahoo.com/table.csv'


    def get(sym, start, end, interval, delegate):
        """
        Get historical data for the given name from yahoo.
        Date format is datetime

        Returns a DataFrame.
        """
        start, end = _sanitize_dates(start, end)
        params = {
            's': sym,
            'a': start.month - 1,
            'b': start.day,
            'c': start.year,
            'd': end.month - 1,
            'e': end.day,
            'f': end.year,
            'g': interval,
            'ignore': '.csv'
        }
        response = requests.get(_URL, params=params)
        status_code = response.status_code
        if status_code == 200:
            dat = response.text

            with Delegator('pandas', delegate) as pd:
                if pd is not None:
                    # Pandas is installed and user want to use it
                    df = pd.read_csv(StringIO(dat))
                    df = df.set_index("Date")
                    return df
                else:
                    # Pandas is not installed or user doesn't want to use it
                    return dat
            del pd

        else:
            raise Exception("status_code=%d - it should be 200" % status_code)

    @click.command()
    @click.option('--delegate/--no-delegate', default=True)
    def main(delegate):
        result = get("AAPL", "2015-04-01", "2015-08-01", "d", delegate)
        print(result)
        print(type(result))

    if __name__ == "__main__":
        main()


    # delegate.py

    class Delegator(object):
        def __init__(self, s_module, delegate):
            self.s_module = s_module
            self.delegate = delegate

        def __enter__(self):
            try:
                if self.delegate:
                    i = __import__(self.s_module, fromlist=[''])
                    return i
                else:
                    return None
            except ImportError:
                return None

        def __exit__(self, *args):
            pass



    # =======


    $ python yahoo_daily.py --no-delegate

@HereticSK Please consider that it is more complex than it seems. You wouldn't be able to simply call `pct_change(period, freq=freq)` with a frequency like '1D', '1W' etc. You also want to provide a trading calendar, which can be represented by a pandas DateOffset. When we want to compute 1 day forward return we actually mean 1 day considering the trading calendar: 1 day forward return computed on Friday will be a 3 days forward return, because there is no trading on Saturday or Sunday (at least on some stock exchanges, but Alphalens is flexible enough to deal with any calendar). For this reason Alphalens store in the `factor_data` columns the pd.Timedelta, which represent the forward return interval and in the `factor_data` index the trading calendar information (pandas DateOffset). Also the code is very flexible and don't ask the user for those details, it infers all the information from the data passed to Alphalens, keeping the API user friendly. 

One last consideration. I wouldn't break the API for now (use your option 3).

> It could be confusing to users already familiar with astropy units and requires more typing (galsim.erg vs u.erg).

I'm not sure why it would be confusing, but to your latter point, the comparison is actually
`galsim.erg` vs `astropy.units.erg`.  The `u.erg` possibility is only because in the quoted example, Josh imported `astropy.units as u`, which would not be something I expect most users to want to do, since `u` is a common variable name, so importing anything `as u` is likely to cause problems.

> The flambda and fnu aliases might be confusing since they usually carry additional units of /(u.cm **2 * u.s), e.g., in IRAF and Wikipedia.

`flambda` and `fnu` are already options for `flux_type`, so for backwards compatibility I thought we should probably preserve their current meaning.  Although I'm sympathetic to the fact that the do often have units of /cm^2/s.  Maybe if the default area and exposure time are 1, then switching to this wouldn't break any existing code...  In which case, probably switching to that would be a good idea.
Perhaps you could make a poll (doodle or whenisgood) to find a day+time combination?

> As for the chip geometry stuff, I think we should try to get LSST DM to create from their code some 
> output files with the necessary information that we could read in from GalSim and create GalSim-
> usable WCS's. 

I agree.

> We might be able to get estimates of the mean Zernike coefficients as a function of position on the 
> fov. If so, then that plus the spider would probably suffice for most purposes here.

Yes, I agree.  This is at least the place to start for any short-term work.

I think I've mostly got the SED class up and running with astropy units.  It's now possible to make an SED as a function of frequency with units of British-Thermal-Units per fortnight per acre:

```python
import galsim
from astropy import units as u
u.imperial.enable()
sed = galsim.SED('1', wave_type=u.Hz, flux_type=u.imperial.BTU/u.fortnight/u.imperial.ac)
```

Next up is enabling dimensionless SEDs, and then integration into chromatic.

@esheldon Did you ever track down the source of this error?  Is there any action item for us to do in the GalSim repo to help address this?  (I did remove the unused and misleading WITH_OPENMP option in our SConstruct file.)

You're right.  I was confused.  I was thinking of the SCAs being the bandpasses rather than the sensors.

So I guess we need a bandpass argument as well.  Indeed, maybe that would be a good choice anyway, since we currently have wavelength_limits to give the range over which the chromatic effects should be implemented.  If we provided a bandpass instead, then these limits would be already implicit from the bandpass, which would be (typically) simpler.

It's starting to seem that probably there is not much advantage to having the PSFs indexed by SCAs, since there are other things that vary too, which are probably equally relevant.  Is there any efficiency gain in calculating them all at once and returning the lot of them as a dict rather than letting the user do it themselves:
```
PSFs_by_SCA = { SCA : galsim.wfirst.getPSF(SCA, approximate_struts=True, bandpass='H158') 
                for SCA in range(1,19) }
```
If not, then maybe we should just have the `getPSF` function return a single PSF and the user could store them into a dict as desired.  E.g. maybe using just a canonical SCA is fine, but they need the different bandpasses, so storing in a dict by bandpass is more useful.

You should add more spaces in the "Options" section, between the option and the descriptions - like this:

{noformat}
Usage:
  smail send    (-t <to>)... [-s <subj>] [-b <body>] [(-a <attach>)...] [(-l <label>)...]
  smail send    -m <msgfile>
  smail draft   (-t <to>)... [-s <subj>] [-b <body>] [(-a <attach>)...] [(-l <label>)...]
  smail draft   -m <msgfile>
  smail insert  (-t <to>)... [-s <subj>] [-b <body>] [(-a <attach>)...] [(-l <label>)...]
  smail insert  -m <msgfile>

Options:
  -t     Recipient address.
  -s     Email subject. Default to "No subject".
  -b     Email body. Default to empty string.
  -a     Attachemnt.
  -l     Label. Default to "smail".
  -m     Use a message file and specify the options in it.
{noformat}

docopt requires at least two spaces between the options and their description, otherwise it parses the options wrong. With one space instead of two or more, you'll also get different behavior from docopt - e.g. you will not have `<msgfile>` in the returned arguments, and you can pass things like "smail send -m=1" and docopt will accept this.

```python

    print(coll)

sys.exit(0)
```

Some context about why subroles even came to exist in the first place:

OpenFisca France has 3 group entities: `famille`, `foyer_fiscal` and `menage`. These entities have  a common structure: one or two people that are "in charge" (typically the parents), and other persons that gravitate around them (typically the children).

One important modelling choice is: do these two people "in charge"  have symmetrical roles? Or is there a clear distinction between them? 

For `menage`, the answer has always been clear for france: the two roles are distinct. I believe this is because INSEE historically loved this idea of "Chef de famille", or "Personne de référence" 👨‍💼 .

For `famille` and `foyer_fiscal`, I'm talking under the control of @benjello but I believe OpenFisca France leant more towards symmetric roles. The way to declare a `famille` has always been something like `{parents: [Alice, Bob], children: [Kevin]}`. Because, _in the model_, there is no reason to distinguish the two parents.

However, practically, indistinguishable parents were challenging in two situations. Both related to projecting values from one group entity to another. This is where subroles came to help.

1. I'm writing a formula for a variable `benefit`, defined for a `famille`. I need to access a variable 
 `code_postal`, defined for a `menage`. But which one of the `famille`'s member `menage` should I use? Having a subrole pointing to _one person_ helped. I could just use: `famille.demandeur.menage('code_postal', period)`, with the convention that the first `parent` of a `famille` has the subrole `demandeur`.

2. I'm writing a formula for a `menage` variable, `disposable_income`, to do some sort of country-level statistics. I'd like to include the `benefits` received by the `famille(s)` inside the `menage`. But I need to be careful not to double count. If two persons are in the same `famille` but in different `menage`, I want to count the `benefits` _only once_. So this is what I do:

```py
benefits_ind = menage.members.famille('benefits', period)
# Only sums for menage members whose role in famille is DEMANDEUR.
# This way, I'm sure that for each famille the benefits are projected on only one menage
benefits_menage = menage.sum(benefits_ind, role = Famille.DEMANDEUR)  
```

So **TL/DR: Modelling pushed for symmetric roles, convenience imposed distinguishable roles. Subroles came as a middle-of-the-road bastard solution that allowed both**

A quick technical note though:

`persons('salary', period)` is a numpy array, but `persons` is **not**. You **cannot** pass it as the `a` argument of a numpy function. 

Numpy arrays are low-level objects. Our `persons` need to be able, among other things, to calculate any variable from the tax and benefit system. A numpy array would not be able to do that 😅 .

So the best we can do  is to change the nature of `persons` so that it _represents_ the concept of an array of persons, but it will not _be_ and array of person.

Thanks for taking the time to review this!

*str deprecation*

AIUI, the options are "don't do it" or "update the interface documentation to deprecate, not disallow". I infer that you'd prefer the former. I'm OK with that. 

*global adapter registry*

Agreed, let's not use it.

*mypy*

AFAICT, it's orthogonal to providing abstract types _and_ to providing adaptation support, so not 100% sure how it's relevant. I'm very happy to use PEP-484 syntax in docstrings though.

*concrete types*

> right now we already define the types - e.g. matchers.Mismatch - so we can allow folk to verify their custom matchers with mypy very very easily if we make sure that our code is annotated, or supply stubs ourselves - without requiring a second identical definition, or even abcs for most of our types. (Where things are abstract, abc is more relevant, but still perhaps overkill (and the global state thing again I don't like).

I disagree that abc is overkill. For me, the whole point of this is to provide abstract types, rather than concrete ones. For example: I'd like to use an immutable implementation of `Mismatch` that doesn't inherit from our mutable implementation. Having structured documentation of the mismatch protocol is extremely useful for doing such a thing. 

It's super important in reducing the amount of inheritance that we have, and in reducing (or at least, documenting) coupling. (e.g. https://github.com/jml/testtools/blob/interfaces/testtools/_itesttools.py#L99 documents what `RunTest` needs of a `TestCase`)
The test failure is not related to this code - note the 0.0000s vs 0.0001s.

@jml one thing in particular that makes testtools matchers useful in contexts other than testtools-based unit tests is this class: https://github.com/rackerlabs/otter/blob/bbb03e94464c7c66df7000a7f48a73a880cb3fe0/otter/test/utils.py#L46

This wraps any matcher in an object supporting __eq__, so you can just use `assertEqual` or `assert ... == ...` with matchers. It provides a `__repr__` that shows the latest mismatch.

traceback2 is a rolling backport of Python3's traceback module. Per https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html - "raise new_exc from original_exc

The expression following from must be an exception or None. It will be set as __cause__ on the raised exception."

The expectation in Python 3 is that __cause__ is either None or a valid exception object - which implies the __traceback__ attribute. The likely thing here is that Django is setting __cause__ (non-standard in Python2) without also setting the exceptions __traceback__ - which it needs to do by hand on Python2 (just as its setting __cause__ by hand)

Of course I am already familiar with Freetype. Its strength - font format abstraction, and try to cope with font error as best effort and keep going silently without stopping - are also some of the reasons why it isn't ideal for CFF charstrings diagnostics: getting at font-format-specific anomalies is difficult, and getting Freetype to abort and complain loudly about font anomalies is difficult. This is also some of the general consideration I have been having about re-implementing the (mostly truetype-centric) rasterization tests via Freetype, so I have thought about it a lot. I hope things will be clearer about how CFF-rasterization tests should go when truetype-rasterization tests is moving along.

Very specifically, `pip wheel` will re-exec `setup.py bdist_wheel` using `sys.executable`.  You get this kind of error:

```
Building wheels for collected packages: sky-metadata
  Running setup.py bdist_wheel for sky-metadata ... error
  Complete output from command /usr/bin/python -u -c "import setuptools, tokenize;__file__='/private/var/folders/bf/hk4tnscx7ps0k06gqgrb8g400000gn/T/pip-oddFVu-build/setup.py';f=getattr(tokenize, 'open', open)(__file__);code=f.read().replace('\r\n', '\n');f.close();exec(compile(code, __file__, 'exec'))" bdist_wheel -d /var/folders/bf/hk4tnscx7ps0k06gqgrb8g400000gn/T/tmpztJmlFpip-wheel-:
  usage: -c [global_opts] cmd1 [cmd1_opts] [cmd2 [cmd2_opts] ...]
     or: -c --help [cmd1 cmd2 ...]
     or: -c --help-commands
     or: -c cmd --help
  
  error: invalid command 'bdist_wheel'
```

This just means you can't run `pip wheel` from within a PEX.  Our work-around is to instead embed virtualenv inside the pex executable, and set up a virtualenv from which to run `pip wheel`.  This has the side benefit of allowing pex library to be pip installed into this temporary virtualenv rather than being embedded, saving about 3MB off the size of the executable.

Hm... one unfortunate downside of frontend tasks operating that way would be that in order to avoid interleaving outputs, `test.pytest` would not be able to operate concurrently with `test.junit` (for example). If inputs were declared upfront (as described earlier in this issue), all of the dependency `@rule`s for each language would already have run by the time their synchronous portions began.

Certainly for something like `./pants test ::`, serializing the various frontend tasks would be a non-starter. Unless I'm still thinking about frontend tasks incorrectly, and per-language support was entirely in `@rule`s, so there was exactly one task that defines `test` ?

@KennethNielsen @ghcs27 I've been making good progress with this, but have hit a stumbling block with the proposal to have two modules **events** and **events_twisted**, each of which would import from a module called **events_base**, with the user explicitly importing from one module or the other.

The problem is that if someone imports **soco.events_twisted** (or imports from **soco.events_twisted**) the python interpreter will, before it does anything else, run the **__init__** module in the soco directory (unless it has already done this).

The **__init__** module has the line:

`from .core import SoCo`

This sets off a chain of imports, including (in soco/services.py", line 43):

`from .events import Subscription`

In other words, any attempt to install from the **events_twisted** module, is pre-empted by **__init__**, which installs from the **events** module.

One brute force method around this is to suggest to users that they rename the **events_twisted.py** file as **events.py**, but that doesn't feel like the right solution. It would mean every upgrade of soco would have to be accompanied by an explicit renaming of files.

The approach I was adopting, of checking whether a twisted reactor is running and then automatically making the appropriate imports based on that, is another simple way around the problem. Any other thoughts or suggestions?

What is happening in this code

```python
   while not self.stop_flag.is_set():
            listener.handle_request()
```
is that `handle_request` calls `socket.select()` behind the scenes, and that waits until the timeout before it returns. Sending a dummy http request to the socket address causes `select` to return earlier, and we don't need to wait for the timeout to expire. The problem of course is that in the scenario @mikix has,  the controller can no longer access the network that the socket is listening on, so the http call never gets through.

The alternatives are (1) to use a polling loop or, `serve_forever` (which is the same thing), or (2) to set a shorter timeout on the socket, or (3) to try and force the socket to close from outside its thread.

Hi! The Debian installer uses Python 3 to run Kolibri, and there is a built-in package `urllib3` in Python 3 that is relatively older than the version we bundled inside Kolibri. This older version of `urllib3` caused the channel not being downloaded fully. 

To confirm that your issue is caused by this older version of urllib3, please try the following commands:
```
> python3
>>> import urllib3
>>> urllib3.__version__
```

The version of your system built-in `urllib3` could probably be 1.15.1, or something older than 1.22, which is the version we bundled inside Kolibri.


We are planning to release 0.7.1 soon that will fix this issue, but I'm not sure if we are going to have a Debian installer for 0.7.1 (@benjaoming, do you know if we will have that? Thank you!).

If you want to use the 0.7.0 debian installer now, my suggestion would be to upgrade your system's `urllib3` by doing:
```
> service kolibri stop
> sudo su kolibri
> pip3 install urllib3==1.22
> service kolibri start
```

Reusing parts of an AMG hierarchy for slightly modified problems is fairly
difficult. In particular, if you change a small part of your initial 
matrix, A,
then it will change the corresponding transfer operators R and P in relevant
rows/columns. Then, when you compute your first coarse-grid matrix,
Ac = R*A*P, this local change becomes a global change in the coarse-grid
operator, and everything from that point on has to be recomputed.

If the changes are quite small, you may be able to precondition A1 with
the AMG hierarchy for A0. Another idea is to use coarse-grid correction
for A0 coupled with a new relaxation based on A1, because relaxation
does not cost anything to set up. How well either of these would work is
highly problem dependent though.

95f8fbc Adds a new feature for wrapper developers to pass keyword arguments into  `xmltodict.parse` and `xmltodict.unparse` in `XMLAdapterMixin` without hardcoding tapioca for individual options coupled to the xmltodict package. This will need some documentation.

XMLAdapterMixin looks for specifically prefixed keywords, removes them from the keyword dictionary that is passed to requests, and passes the suffixes and values directly to xmltodict.parse / unparse as keyword arguments. There are two special prefixes `"xmltodict_parse__"` and `"xmltodict_unparse__"`. These prefixes could be anything we choose, I added the module name and double underscore for readability and to be explicit.

The wrapper developer can set these parameters inside of their `get_request_kwargs` like this example that removes the XML declaration tag from POST output:

*tapioca_gsheets.py/GsheetsClientAdapter/get_request_kwargs:*
```
kwargs['xmltodict_unparse__full_document'] = False
```

This approach allows the wrapper developer to pass any parameter they need into xmltodict.parse or unparse without limitation to control input and output. [See xmltodict source for options](https://github.com/martinblech/xmltodict/blob/master/xmltodict.py)

was first to register the name coursera-dl on PyPI, which meant that I was

    pip install coursera

Note the lack of the `-dl` suffix above. This is the only place were we are
called coursera only and not coursera-dl (OK, and the main module of our
project, but that's generally not important for users).

first and then install our project:

    pip uninstall coursera-dl
    pip install --upgrade coursera

Or, better yet, use a virtual environment with the latest version that I
have released on PyPI:

    pip install --upgrade --pre coursera

This is actually correct - `_generate_valid_antecedents` is computing valid _indices_ with respect to the spans which survive the pruning stage. Each span can be coreferent with something occurring _before_ it, but for the first span, there is no other span before it, so it can only predict the null class (which is appended later, so not considered here). And for the second span, it can only predict the first span, etc etc. So here, we are effectively selecting indices which for a given span position in _predicted_antecedents_, it can predict. 

The mask is an additive log mask (in the sense that it is added to a tensor rather than multiplied with it), which is why it is all zeros and `-inf`s.

> I don't know at what level MEI places instruments in a Stream. The MEI-to-music21 converter does not put instruments in the same place as the MusicXML converter. The current `locate` behaviour matches with `imshow`, which puts integer coordinates at pixel *centers*. You can test that with:

    import numpy as np
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    frame = np.zeros((10, 10))
    frame[4, 4] = 1
    plt.imshow(frame)
    plt.plot(4, 4, marker='o')
    plt.show()

The produced plot has the marker centered in the pixel.
A Gaussian symmetric around pixel (5, 5) should be found at location (5, 5). The origin of trackpy (and imshow) is at the center of the topleft pixel. This is how it is tested now, I think it is ok to mention it in the docstring of locate.

> Note that record-based output should be consistent with aligning the value or not. "globus bookmark show" aligns the values so that they all start at the same column. But "globus whoami --verbose" does not, as it just has a single space between the : and the value.

Yes, that was a bug in `whoami`. The fix will go out in the next CLI release.

> I assume the rule is that multi-record output uses the table format, where-as single-record output uses the Name:Value output. I think we should be more consistent about this.
>
> I suggest we name each of these output format distinctly: "--format table" vs "--format record". For the record based output, if there are multiple output records, have a blank link between each record.

Yes, tabular data is produced as a table, and single records are colon-delimited.

I don't see this behavior as inconsistent, and think that adding more formatting options only serves to confuse things and make more work for us.
The purpose of `--format TEXT` is to be free-form text data which we produce. It only needs to exist to describe non-`--format JSON` output.

If we have `--format TABLE` and `--format RECORD` we'll still need `--format TEXT` to describe things like `globus ls`, `globus bookmark locate`, `globus task wait`, and `globus whoami` output.

I don't see what we gain by shoehorning non-tabular data into a table or tabular data into colon-delimited output.


The nice thing about `--format TEXT` and `--format JSON` is that _every single command_ should support them. In some cases, this is a cop-out because the text output is just JSON, but that's something we're working to improve. There are a few misses (`task wait` and `whoami` don't support `JSON`, which I'm about to file a bug about), but the intent is that these formats are global, so that you don't have to check what formats a command supports before requesting output.

```

Now, later you look for those motifs inside the individual time series `a, b, c`, so you will have to normalize them, but you do that individually, so

a = normalize(a)
n = count_motif(a, m)

* If we want to introduce the sliding window for `headers_by_height`, we still need to figure out the size of sliding window of `headers_by_height`. I have a similar need when generating a wagon archive to include in a larger installation bundle, but came up with a workaround I'm happy with, which is to create a minimal `dev/setup.py` file, then run:

    wagon create -r locked-requirements.txt -f -t tar.gz dev

The minimal `setup.py` file is just:

```
"""Python pseudo-package for install bundle generation"""

from distutils.core import setup

setup(
    name="my_company_python_deploy_bundle",
    version="1",
    description="Pseudo-package for Python install bundle generation",
    author="<My company> <My project> development team",
    packages=[],
)
```

This is enough to give `wagon` the info it needs to generate the bundle filename (i.e. `my_company_python_deploy_bundle-1-...`) without needing to define a full publishable Python package.

I very much appreciate this thread.  I tend to agree with Peter that the
"least surprising" or --minimal is better from my standpoint.  As to
developers wanting the latest....I'm using AS to install production code at
JPM and offer some thoughts:

1.  I recently went through my py-conda_requires.txt DOWNGRADING all
package versions to match exactly what is found in Anaconda 2.0.1 .  That
and only that Anaconda release is  a  "SEAL" approved component at JPM.
Sure, we'll get newer anaconda releases similarly approved in the system
but there will be an absolute requirement that packages not approved will
not be allowed into "production".   BTW: anaconda is a developers salvation
here because there is a menu of 150+ packages to choose from without
jumping through the security hoops we face.

2. I currently deploy by dealing with all of the versioning in QA and UAT
(pre-production) environments and a final requirements file is deployed
(--no-deps) with the exact version and build that was tested.

3. Aaron's B2.0 or B3.0 question above.  B2.0 is preferred.  B3.0 is more
likely to have it's own dependencies that will want to upgrade C3.0.

conda anticipates my needs and get's 90% of what I expect.  I have,
however, always regarded the over-agressive updates of conda as something
that I have to explain away.  Most people have no idea of pinning which I
consider an advanced management feature.  More common case is updating one
package and setting off an avalanche of apparently unrelated updates.

Truth be told. As AS is better understood, this will be less of a
"production" problem since we can have AS configurations that only contain
security scanned and "SEAL" (or similar) approved packages.

--
Phil

Hi,

The problem is that when any update of a set of packages, which includes
among it an upgrade of conda itself, is unsuccessful, then conda is removed
and the entire installation is corrupted. Thus, there is no 'conda' command
in:

miniconda/bin

So far the only way that I have found to fix this, is to re-install
miniconda and then reinstall each and every package individually again. So
I was wondering if there was an easier way? For recent installations, this
is not that much of a problem, however as additional tweaks get made to a
current anaconda installed python (ie custom packages, configurations,
etc), it becomes rather a pain to re-install everything. So apart from
regular backups is there an alternative?

P

If you run "conda config --show" you will see there is a configuration parameter callled "envs_dirs". It normally points to the location where you installed conda, and the envs subdir in it. It also points to $HOME/.conda/envs as well. By default creating a conda environment add it to one of those places. If you create it in a non-standard location you have to manually add the the new environment to the end_dirs list. The way to do it is via conda off couse (or you can do it with any text editor)
conda config --add envs_dirs <directory_where_you_installed_the_new_environment>
this will add that to the "env_dirs" list inside your $HOME/.condarc file.

If you look at the compatibility matrix, you'll see that Elasticsearch 5.0 is fully supported with all current actions and filters. The elasticsearch-py module, version 2.4, supports all necessary API calls to allow Curator to work with Elasticsearch versions 2.0 through 5.0. 

The python module version shift you saw is because version 5.0 of the python module has some API changes that caused integration tests to fail. When Curator v5 comes out it will be refactored to use the v5 python module.

This repo follows the convention where `pip install elasticsearch` is for the most recent version of Elasticsearch. 

When Elastic releases a new major version of Elasticsearch, we will update the package so that `pip install elasticsearch` will be for that new version. 

We will also create a branch for the previous major version, and release a new package on PyPI for the previous major version. 

So any release of `elasticsearch6` will be at the time when Elasticsearch 7.x comes out. As of now, it's not "imminent".

@saulcruz By default, Python does not set the `tzinfo` (too dangerous and not obvious). But if you do it yourself, `isoformat` will do it:
```python
In [8]: datetime.datetime(2017,10,10,12,45,16,tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc).isoformat()
Out[8]: '2017-10-10T12:45:16+00:00'

In [10]: datetime.datetime(2017,10,10,12,45,16).isoformat()
Out[10]: '2017-10-10T12:45:16'
```

Note that the SDK install python-dateutil, which can help as well:
```python
In [11]: from dateutil.tz import tzlocal
In [13]: datetime.datetime(2017,10,10,12,45,16,tzinfo=tzlocal()).isoformat()
Out[13]: '2017-10-10T12:45:16-07:00'
```

Note that the last one will use the tz of the system. It's nice for a desktop machine, usually more dangerous for a VM or a server.

I'm doing a full run with `openaddr-process` now too. Doing this has convinced me we want to use `multiprocessing` for job management, not `threading`. If we switch to that interrupting runaway jobs gets easy. No more getting mired in some ancient ESRI server.

Your signal code is promising; I note it uses multiprocessing. Good thing, apparently [Python does not support](https://docs.python.org/2/library/signal.html#module-signal) delivering signals to child threads. (Linux sort of does, but POSIX signals+threads are notorious.)

Signals are the right way with multiprocessing. `Process.terminate()` just sends a `SIGTERM` to the subprocess, just like killing from the command line. I think that will be sufficient for aborting a broken job, no need to install custom signal handlers or anything.

Some useful configuration parameters:
* Individual POST size limits.
* Cumulative concurrent POST size limits.
* Concurrent connection limits. Allow the kernel to queue new connections and eventually refuse them when it grows too large (kernel queue size controlled by net.core.somaxconn sysctl on Linux). 
* For the connections that have been accepted, limit accepting new connections from the same client address, with separate limits to trigger queuing or rejecting of new connections from the same client address.
* Limit the combined number of concurrently accepted connections from all clients.

It sounds like you're trying an import in two different installations of Python, or two different environments. Check `sys.executable` to see which Python and environment you're running in, and `sys.path` to see where it looks to import modules.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

For more detail, see [this blog post](http://takluyver.github.io/posts/i-cant-import-it.html).

Well, the BoundaryAttack tires to find an initial (possibly very large) adversarial perturbation to start with (to minimize further). By default (if nothing else is specified), it does so by drawing a random input and usually that one will have the wrong class. If you only have two classes, this will only work 50% of time (or maybe even less often your classes aren't balanced, etc.).

There are many ways to fix this. For example you could manually pass a starting_point to the BoundaryAttack:

`img_adv = attack(img, label[0], verbose=True, starting_point=...)`

Such a starting_point should have the same format as your input image; it should basically just be an image from another class.

Before you do any of that you should however make sure that it's really necessary. The BlendedUniformNoiseAttack used as initialization tries 1000 times and in principle (if your bounds are set correctly) that should also work for two classes. Could it be that random noise images are always classified as the same class? It that case, the starting_point approach as described above should help

So, it seems the new import algorithm is slower to start but faster otherwise. The slow start is mainly due to the setting up of the context container.

I optimized the context object a bit, and now the situation looks like this:

testcase                 | dev branch (functional) | refactor (functional) | refactor (Model)
------------------------ | -----------------------: | --------------------: | ----------------:
model with 2 fields      | FASTEST                  | +9.8%                 | +26.5%
model with 10 fields     | +13.6%                   | FASTEST               | +4.7%
model with submodels | +101.4%                  | FASTEST               | +0.8%

The point where the two branches perform equally with flat models is approximately 4 fields per model.

Inefficiencies in the recursion behavior of the old algorithm make it extremely slow as soon as you add even just one submodel.

> do you claim that the JVP of the VJP requires 2N memory?
Precisely, yes. I agree that jvp(f)'s memory usage doesn't scale with N.

I previously thought exactly what you've just described was how it worked, I only revised my thoughts after I'd already finished writing forward mode and was surprised by the performance of the new hvps.

To address your questions, grad(f) does a forward pass and a reverse pass. If you do jvp(grad(f)), then you're going to be forward differentiating the whole of grad, both forward pass (i.e. f itself) as well as the reverse pass. Those w's are the forward derivatives of the forward pass.

It sounds like your suggesting a graph like this (have I got the arrows in the right direction?)
```
jvp(vjp(fun))  u_1 <-- u_2 <-- v
                ^       ^      ^
                |       |      |
               u_1 <-- u_2 <-- v
                ^       ^      ^
                |       |      |
fun            x_1 --> x_2 --> y
```
Which looks like you're just forward differentiating the reverse pass. Can you do that?

If you decompose grad into the forward pass and the reverse (vjp) pass, you could forward differentiate just the reverse vjp pass w.r.t. v, but this would just yield the gradient. I don't think you can forward differentiate it w.r.t. x_1 because the vjp isn't directly a function of x_1.

In reply to some of @dougalm's points:

The inability to mix NumPy and CuPy arrays is not because of any approach,
it's inherent to the libraries themselves. You can't operate on both data
types at the same time. Returning a NumPy array by default in places will
often just crash.

If you want to be backend agnostic, the correct approach to empty container
spaces would probably be to special case them and return empty lists
instead of empty NumPy arrays when calling flatten, ones, etc. on them.
It's the only way of being agnostic.

That the lib approach requires wrapped libraries to have the same API is
what allows you to avoid code duplication. If you want to wrap a library
that has a different API, you can write a super thin wrapper around it.

Overall, I haven't really followed the back and forth between flatten, but
from a software design perspective it seems like a bad idea to duplicate
logic for different backends. It's a matter of time before the code will
rot.

The performance aspect seems easy enough to address. You can write a custom
"lib" function that acts like "vspace(x).lib" with almost no overhead.

Tool Shed artifact linting means...

 - Pretty much full XSD validation of ``repository_dependencies.xml`` files (at the top of shed repositories).
 - Partial XSD validation of ``tool_dependencies.xml`` files (at the top of shed repositories).
 - Checking README files ( #89 ) ( #91 ) (at the top of shed repositories)
 - Basic checks of ``.shed.yml`` 
   - is your repository name and owner valid
   - is the category extant
   - does the name match best practice (package_ for tool_dependency_definitions, etc...).

Can track progress on this at - https://github.com/galaxyproject/planemo/issues/107

Basically the goal would be to ensure ``shed_create`` and ``shed_upload`` commands will work after linting. Obviously there is still a lot that linting is not catching - but it has caught some stuff (markdown readme files, malformed ``tool_dependencies.xml``, mis-named repositories).

Assigning to '_' is pretty common (I say that with ~20 years dev time). This tells anyone reading the code that you are calling a function that normally returns something useful that people want, but that, in this particular (and typically unusual) edge case, you are discarding the result. This is a way to highlight an unusual use case, and that is never a bad thing.

This debate about whether assigning to '_' is correct or not misses the point - this _is_ a convention which _does_ exist, so imo it should be supported.

I am definitely with @tgoodlet on the `implementer` term, also because that term is already established and hinted at in the naming scheme (`hookimpl`, `HookImplMarker`) which the implementer then uses. So implementer is the way to go on that side IMO.

Leaves us with the other side. That seems to be the harder part, because if we would stick with internal names, we'd end up with a `specifier` and that just sounds odd - and to be clear: I am talking about naming roles for people here. A person called `specifier` sounds more like a biologist discovering new types of bugs and naming them. Discovering bugs is something we have in common with biologists, but we wouldn't want to be called specifier, would we? :D

As @tgoodlet said: there is already established terminlogy, so lets have a look in Wikipedia:

> The host application provides services which the plug-in can use, including a way for plug-ins to register themselves with the host application and a protocol for the exchange of data with plug-ins.

So the program is called `host` - that sounds right for the program, but I am not sure if calling the person working on that should be called `hoster`, but at least it's not terrible I guess, but maybe provider would be better?

> Programmers typically implement plug-in functionality using shared libraries, which get dynamically loaded at run time, installed in a place prescribed by the host application.

Programmers implement plugin functionality and a pogrammer doing that, is in the role of the implementer. Ease. See above.

So at the moment I lean towards hoster and implementer. I updated the PR to mirror that, so you can read it again and see how that rings.

> In this case I'm pretty sure I do not want tzname() returning unicode in Python 2. I'm 90% sure that would break a bunch of stuff downstream.

I agree.

I did a bit more digging to try to see a bit more clear.  It seems like `tzlocal` and `tzwinlocal` have different problems even if the symptoms are the same.

`tzlocal` fetches its info from `time.tzname` which is a byte string containing non-ASCII data in CP1252 (because it uses a legacy ANSI API).  The problem comes from our attempt to `.encode('ascii')` this value.  

We have a few options for `tzlocal`:
1) pass it on as is and let the caller deal with it 
2) try to coerce it to ASCII (e.g. using `Unidecode` or other lossy algorithm).

`tzwinlocal` fetches its info from the `SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\TimeZoneInformation` registry key.  It (correctly) reads the `StandardName` and `DaylightName` as Unicode strings.  The problem is it has non-ASCII data.

We have a more options options for obtaining a byte string from `tzwininfo`:
1) pass it on as is (creates situations where `tzinfo.tzname()` returns Unicode -- inconsistent at best);
2) switch to ANSI versions of registry API (possibly lossy, but consistent with `time.tzname`);
3) encode as UTF-8 (lossless, but inconsistent with `time.tzname`).

I can't think of anything else at the moment.  Let me know what you prefer.

To clarify, `MarkerCluster` is a plugin and is not available in the main folium module. So you cannot use it as `folium.MarkerCluster`. Instead you have to import the plugins and get it from there. Two options:

```
import folium.plugins
folium.plugins.MarkerCluster()
```

or

```
from folium.plugins import MarkerCluster
MarkerCluster()
```

@SimeonKostadinoff I was able to reproduce the issue with 0.26.1. After looking at the client code, it seems that we're sending metadata to the server twice. I think that is the issue. You can see in this snippet that we're sending metadata twice, which I think is triggering the `too many parts` error on the server side. You can see the 

```python
return self.request(method='POST',
                            url=url_string,
                            params=params,
                            data=metadata,
                            files={'file': file_tuple,
                                   'metadata': (None,
                                                json.dumps(metadata),
                                                'application/json')},
                            accept_json=True)
```

If you remove the `data=metadata`, you shouldn't see the error anymore. I've verified that this is no longer an issue in the latest release, i.e. 1.0.2, so you can try using that as well.

@tommyjcarpenter 

1. You are not supposed to `source` anything.
2. `~/` is not going to work. Normally path `"~/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/powerline/bindings/fish"` means literally `./~/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/powerline/bindings/fish`, `~/` is not expanded in double quotes. This is OK in first statement because there are no quotes, but by using `""` you explicitly told fish that you do not need some operations, including `~/` expansion.

   You may check that this works as I said yourself by running `fish` using `strace -e open,stat -o /tmp/fish.strace fish`, this will show you lots of entries like

        stat("~/.vam/powerline/powerline/bindings/fish/set.fish", 0x7ffded2eb9b0) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)

    Note: `stat()` syscall *does not* expand `~/`, it is shell (and some other apps that copied this behaviour because it is convenient) own feature.

I have just

    set fish_function_path "$HOME/.vam/powerline/powerline/bindings/fish" $fish_function_path
    powerline-setup

in my `config.fish` (basically because I am not using fish, I am testing powerline there): note `$HOME`, not `~/`.

> The path addings work because powerline-setup gets defined.

`powerline-setup` gets defined because you are running `source`, path additions have nothing to do with this.

Which docs are you looking at?  The quote you have looks like it's from an older version of our docs.  The [current tutorial section on waiting for a job](http://drmaa-python.readthedocs.org/en/latest/tutorials.html#waiting-for-a-job) makes this pretty clear:

> After a job completes, it leaves behind accounting information, such as exit status and usage, until either `wait()` or `synchronize()` with `dispose` set to `True` is called. It is the responsibility of the application to make sure one of these two functions is called for every job. Not doing so creates a memory leak. Note that calling `synchronize()` with `dispose` set to `True` flushes all accounting information for all jobs in the list. If you want to use `synchronize()` and still recover the accounting information, set `dispose` to `False` and call `wait()` for each job.

What you can see in qfbv_tactic.cpp is the code that constructs a tactic from other tactics, e.g., and_then, using_params, mk_* all construct the individual tactics). In most cases their names are the same as the names you would use to construct tactics in an SMT2 file, with minor syntactic adjustments. 

The parallel SAT solver is a portfolio, so it runs multiple configurations of a sequential solver in parallel and it exchanges clauses between them; I think that's only units at the moment, @NikolajBjorner knows more. One thing about portfolios is that they become non-deterministic (without explicit measures to prevent it) and there is no way to predict their "usual" behavior, so in terms of performance you'd likely have to run it on your problems and see what average speedup you get. Evaluating this on other problems would probably not give you figures that you can rely on. However, it's often the case that you do get fairly good speedups, in ideal cases even close to linear (see SAT competitions etc). 

par_or and par_and have been there for a long time, but there are currently no plans to use them in the default tactics. And further, there are currently no plans for doing anything in parallel by default, because at this point there are too many users that use Z3 without parallelism and they parallelize on the higher level, as they usually have more than one problem to solve. Running parallel tactics inside of Z3 in this kind of setup wouldn't work very well.

theano.printing.debugprint(fn1)

 |GpuFromHost [@B] ''   7

 |   |Elemwise{eq,no_inplace} [@D] ''   5

# cast loss to floatX before:
fn2 = theano.function([a,b], togpu(l.astype(theano.config.floatX).mean()))
theano.printing.debugprint(fn2)
GpuElemwise{TrueDiv}[(0, 0)] [@A] ''   8
 |GpuCAReduce{add}{1} [@B] ''   7
 | |GpuFromHost [@C] ''   6
 |   |Elemwise{Composite{Cast{float32}(EQ(i0, i1))}} [@D] ''   5
 |     |MaxAndArgmax.1 [@E] 'argmax'   3
 |     | |HostFromGpu [@F] ''   1
 |     | | |<CudaNdarrayType(float32, matrix)> [@G]
 |     | |TensorConstant{(1,) of 1} [@H]
 |     |b [@I]
 |GpuFromHost [@J] ''   4
   |Elemwise{Cast{float32}} [@K] ''   2
     |Shape_i{0} [@L] ''   0
       |<CudaNdarrayType(float32, matrix)> [@G]
```

So actually, `T.argmax` and `T.eq` is performed on the CPU, even if we pretend you want the end result on the GPU. Summarization happens on CPU for the first variant, and on GPU for the second. In any case, things are copied back to GPU for dividing the sum by the number of data points.

I guess we should leave everything as it is. If you want to compute an accuracy, you probably want to output it, so you need it on CPU and it's totally fine to compute it in float64 precision instead of copying things back to GPU. If you want to compute something you want to calculate a gradient of, you probably want to cast the loss to float32. We can think about adding a `dtype` argument to `mean()` once there is a use case that needs it, but I think we shouldn't change it just in case somebody has the float64 warning enabled.
