Update:

1. `lasagne.updates.get_or_compute_grads()` now checks whether all params are shared variables. I'm not completely happy with this, but adding another function called in every `lasagne.updates.*` function seemed stupid as well. Note that with this change you can't use `get_or_compute_grads()` to compute gradients for things that are not shared variables, and that you can't use `sgd()` or anything else to compute updates for things that are not shared variables (but except for `sgd()`, all of them expect shared variables anyway, as they use `get_value()` to check the shape for creating accumulators).
2. The recurrent layers bypassed `create_param()` if `hid_init` or `cell_init` was a `TensorVariable`, and expected it to include the batch dimension if and only if it was a `TensorVariable`. Now that `create_param()` accepts any Theano expressions, I figured a better way to distinguish the two cases was via the dimensionality -- if it has enough dimensions to include the batch dimension, it probably includes the batch dimension, and if it has one dimension less, it will be replicated via a dot product against a vector of ones (as before). This required some more changes:
   (a) `create_param()` currently cannot be told to accept either a parameter of `(num_units)` or of `(batchsize, num_units)`. I've solved this by calling it a second time with the second shape if the first call failes (for which I changed `create_param()` to raise a `ValueError`, not a `RuntimeError`, before these were mixed).
   (b) `batchsize` is not usually known in a recurrent layer, only `num_units` is. I've solved this by changing `create_param()` to also accept `None` in its shape specification.
   Note that all this makes the recurrent layers incompatible with previous versions, because in previous versions `hid_init` was initialized to have a batch size of 1 for some reason, while now I initialize it to not include the batch size at all (otherwise we can't use `ndim` to know whether it needs replication over the batch or not).
  And again, I'm not completely happy with this. Maybe it would be nicer to change `create_param` to accept a set of alternative shapes that are also acceptable? The original, primary shape would be passed to a callable if a callable was given, and it would not be allowed to contain `None`s, but all other shapes may be underspecified and are only used to check whether a given shared variable, Theano expression or numpy array is accepted.

Any input or questions are welcome!

> Note that if you actually want to change it you can just override the `compute_input` method in the subclass.

Ah, didn't see it because you were not using it in this occasion: https://github.com/Botev/Lasagne/blob/recurrent/lasagne/layers/recurrent.py#L133

> But I agree, after today looking more at the code, that the what I call `StepLayer` should be any layer that takes some inputs and produce many outputs,

`StepLayer` does not need to be a single layer taking multiple inputs and producing multiple outputs, it could also be a graph of multiple input layers, processing layers and output layers (so you can reuse `Conv2DLayer`, for example).
Thinking again, of course `StepLayer` *could* be a special class if we want to, with implementations for standard cases as you have them, and a separate `CustomStepLayer` class that encapsulates an arbitrary Lasagne graph of layers. That would push some of the necessary complexity from `RecurrentStepLayer` into `CustomStepLayer`, and it could make it easier to keep the current performance optimizations such as stacked weights for the LSTM. It would probably make it less obvious how to implement a convolutional LSTM, though.

> while the `RecurrentStepLayer` should just be there for the scan.

Exactly. Its only purpose is to encapsulate the recurrence, so to `lasagne.layers.get_output()` everything still looks like a DAG for a feed-forward network.

> What would we need to figure out for that to work?

To answer my own question: Well, we'd need to figure out the interface from the container (`RecurrentStepLayer`) to the contained thing (`StepLayer`, or something), and the interface from the container to the outside. The new implementation should allow N >= 0 input sequences mapped to M > 0 output sequences, and it should allow fixed initial hidden states, learned initial hidden states or initial hidden states fed in from (an)other layer(s). It should provide an easy way to implement vanilla RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs, convolutional RNNs, convolutional LSTMs/GRUs, and anything else. If possible, it should allow to stack multiple LSTMs/GRUs in a single container. Am I missing anything?

Writing either ASCII or Unicode from both Python 2 and 3 works with h5py. The bug you are refering to  seems to be specific to the case when you use the create_dataset method without specifying a data type, however, the following variants for writing strings work fine:

```
>>> dset.attrs["name_utf8"] = u"Hello"
```

or:

```
>>> dt = h5py.special_dtype(vlen=unicode)
>>> dset = f.create_dataset("name", (100,), dtype=dt)
```

The issue is that the following has different behaviors in Python 2/3:

```
>>> dt = h5py.special_dtype(vlen=str)      # In Python 2 this is ASCII in Python 3 this is UTF8
>>> dset = f.create_dataset("name", (100,), dtype=dt)
```

Similarly

```
dset.attrs["name] = "Hello"    # In Python 2 this is ASCII and in Python 3 this is UTF8
```
To ensure consistent behavior across Python 2/3 and enable users outside of the US to use the format, we should use UTF8 encoding on write in Python 2.

I'm hesitant around this… 

Python packaging & virtual environments are complicated enough and extremely confusing to people. 

I don't know enough about how virtualenv works to know whether that would be an ok thing to do… with conda I don't know how it would treat `pip` . If we were going to do this it might be safer to do a `python -m pip` rather than plain `pip` the executable.

Also, this doesn't give them any insight as to how to uninstall it (which we should probably add to the README now that I think about it). Importantly, it wouldn't give you any idea of how to remove the configuration file (though of course… right now we don't mention this in the README either so that could be solved). 

Also, I think it'd be nicer if people could learn about this app exclusively as "nteract_on_jupyter" and not "jupyter-extension", but that's going to require a more general renaming approach than can be fixed right now (which I think we talked about).

All that said…  if we want people to install from the root of the repo they could do 
`pip install -e  applications/jupyter-extension` … but they'd still need to run the serverextension enabling command. Is the key win here being able to run this from the root of the repo or only having one command to run for both?

We could put a config file somewhere for them as part of the install (at `<your env path>/etc/jupyter/jupyter_notebook_config.d/nteract_on_jupyter.json` even though `setuptools` won't handle that itself with the `data_files` argument… but I don't know if there are any hooks in `pip uninstall` to then go back and remove it later (which is what a non-editable `pip uninstall nteract_on_jupyter` will do via the setuptools data_files logic).

关键是在调用者那边是如何用的。这些内容在调用者那边基本都是对象。如果不是返回对象，那调用者不得不封装这个json。另外，在我看来，json 是数据格式，不是实体，而是实体的一种表现。json 的作用在于存储，但缺少操作的能力。或许我们可以搞一些 serializer 去输出不同的格式，或者在对象上提供输出这些格式的方法。

[this may go beyond the scope of the PR btw!]

Personally, I think we should explicitly **NOT** have an index cache available if the job is fully loaded simply because then we've got explicit duplication of data and this could lead to similar problems of which is being read. I would say that either:

- We have just one _dict (or whatever) and a flag that says it's fully loaded or not. If you request data outside the lazy loaded data, it triggers a full load, changes the flag and overwrites the cache.
-  We have two dictionaries but we explicitly blank the cache if the Job is fully loaded.

We then restrict the lazy loading/caching to:

- Ganga start up: If the index cache is present on disk, load that. If not fully load the job
- When anything changes in the job, fully load it (or automatically fully load it from monitoring) and blank the cache (both in memory and on disk) to avoid confusion
- At Ganga shutdown, any fully loaded jobs get written out along with their index cache.

That's my 2 cents anyway :)

I guess I wouldn't be averse to something like `PATCH /v1/buckets/my-bucket/collections/my-collection/records?status=open&_limit=1`. But we don't support it now, and I am extremely hesitant to endorse the use of Kinto as a queue. I think using a queue as a queue is going to be better for you than using a database as a queue. I don't know if this is still a problem, but I had a co-worker from a couple jobs ago who worked for a company who had built a queue around MongoDB and constantly ran into issues where the database indices gradually got less and less efficient as records moved forward in time. I think they ended up having to rebuild indices periodically.

My personal opinion about kinto-redis is that it doesn't support the level of query you need in order to avoid race conditions. I'd expect the same thing from kinto-mongodb. But maybe for some applications that's OK? I guess if losing your data were a big problem, you wouldn't be using MongoDB anyhow.

It looks like PR introduced confusion as to what a language is.  Sessions and clients have a concept of language enumerated in the Constants: scala or python.  However, _send_command is expecting a language of sql or default. This leads to the magic crashing when I don't specify a language when using %%sparkmagic.  It's also strange that the user specifies language multiple times: first when creating the endpoint, and again each time that they run the %%sparkmagic. This semantic overloading is confusing and leading to bugs. We should find a different term for whether SQL is being used or not, and a better way for the user to specify it.

We have use cases where the radio needs to be enabled to communicate IPv6 link-local messages while Thread is disabled.  The separation between [`otIp6SetEnabled`](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/ip6.h#L67) and [`otThreadSetEnabled`](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/thread.h#L78) allows us to support those use cases.  When we desire to disable the radio completely, we disable both IPv6 and Thread.  Is this a workable solution for you?

![2016-08-zj5890t-example](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2080552/17929615/e98e3c5a-6a44-11e6-87e5-63a621efb62f.png)

Code:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
from escpos import printer

# A wrapper to organise item names & prices into columns
class item:
    def __init__(self, name="", price="", dollarSign=False):
        self.name=name
        self.price=price
        self.dollarSign=dollarSign

    def encode(self):
        rightCols = 6
        leftCols = 24
        if(self.dollarSign):
            leftCols = leftCols / 2 - rightCols / 2
        left = self.name.ljust(leftCols)
        sign = "$" if self.dollarSign else ""
        right = (sign + self.price).rjust(rightCols)
        return (left + right + "\n").encode()

# Information for the receipt
items = [
    item(name="Example item #1", price="4.00"),
    item(name="Another thing", price="3.50"),
    item(name="Something else", price="1.00"),
    item(name="A final item", price="4.45")
]
subtotal = item(name="Subtotal", price="12.95")
tax = item(name="A local tax", price="1.30");
total = item(name="Total", price="14.25", dollarSign=True);

# Date is kept the same for testing
date = "Monday 6th of April 2015 02:56:25 PM";

# Start the printer
p = printer.File("/dev/usb/lp0")

# Print top logo
p.set(align="center")
p.image("escpos-php.png", impl="bitImageColumn")

# Name of shop
p.set(align="center", width=2)
p.text("ExampleMart Ltd.\n");
p.set(align="center")
p.text("Shop No. 42.\n")
p.text("\n")

# Title of receipt
p.set(align="center", text_type="B")
p.text("SALES INVOICE\n");

# Items
p.set(align="left", text_type="B")
p.text(item(name="", price="$"));
p.set(align="left")
for item in items:
    p.text(item);
p.set(text_type="B")
p.text(subtotal);
p.text("\n")

# Tax and total
p.set()
p.text(tax);
p.set(width=2);
p.text(total);

# Footer
p.text("\n\n")
p.set(align="center")
p.text("Thank you for shopping at\nExampleMart.\n");
p.text("For trading hours, please visit example.com\n");
p.text("\n\n")
p.text(date + "\n");

# Cut the paper
p.cut()
```

Hi. PyPY dev here. I am slowly looking into the issues raised. About "PyPy doesn't seem to register class docstrings for some reason" - once PyType_Ready is called, no changes to the c PyTypeObject struct will be reflected into app-level python.  About reference counting, we do not support sys.getrefcount() at all and do not promise to collect objects that have been freed. We will not support using __del__ as a mechanism to do anything but free resources.

And in more detail:

We create an internal app-level object when PyType_Ready is called that reflects the c-level object, we track the refcount of the c-level object and when it goes to 0 may delete both objects at our convenience but our garbage collection is a mark-and-sweep scheme, however if there is no memory pressure we may choose to not collect anything. When we do call the __del__ method on objects, it may be during the execution of exit() from the interpreter.

We do not currently have a mechanism to update the app-level object from the c-level object (except in some special cases) after PyType_Ready is called.

Ninos: you can specify a single cert with `certbot certificates --cert-name example.tld` to get information about a single certificate.

1. No. There is no command like this at this time.
2. The command you listed in step 1 actually causes Certbot to exit with an error because it can't parse its arguments (`--expand` doesn't take any arguments). Assuming you meant `certbot --expand --cert-name my-cert -d d.com,e.com`, you're correct that this command causes the existing to domains to be removed from my-cert.
3. (and 4) I think making `--expand` additive only would be a good idea, but as you suggested in question 4, the user would also have to include `--cert-name`. Otherwise, if the user just ran `certbot --expand -d <new-domains>` we wouldn't know which cert to expand! The problem with implementing this though is it will change the behavior of existing commands for our users which we're trying to avoid right now. `--expand` predates `--cert-name` by quite a bit.

I personally think the best path forward for documenting this stuff is to make the minor changes to the `--expand` documentation as suggested at https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/3664#issuecomment-294013607. It is indeed true that clf.connect() continuously polls for a tag, after all there is no other way to find one. The default polling strategy is to sense the set of rdwr['targets'] for 5 times with an interval of 500ms and then check the terminate condition. This continues until terminate becomes True. During the 500ms (minus the time it was polling) there is be no RF field generated.

The polling strategy is set through the iterations and interval options that clf.connect() uses when calling clf.sense(). Thus one way to influence polling is to use clf.sense() API from application code. Other ways are to either add delay in the terminate callback function (that's of course a bit of a hack), or patch the nfc/clf/__init__.py file to use different values.

Finally, since a couple of minutes the version on the git master branch will allow the 'iterations' and 'interval' to be set as rdwr options.

Preethi, with "there are other options" do you mean the three options pointed out in that article:

* maintaining a python 2 base and using 2to3 to generate the python 3 code
* maintaining a python 3 base and using 3to2 to generate the python 2 code
* maintaining a code base that runs on both python 2 and 3

If so, we should go for the last of these options, IMO, and that's what the current code base does. The first two approaches do not cost less, and they have their own complexities (e.g. limitations of these tools).

With that last option, the `__future__` imports are sometimes needed to enable compatibility.

The difficulty with the `print_function` future is that even when it is not imported, python 2 has a `print()` function. What the `print_function` future does is to disable the `print` statement.

I found that sometimes in python 2 the `print()` function behaves differently when importing the `print_function` future compared to when not importing it. I did not investigate why that happens and what the differences were, but the ones I ran into were such that my code did not work. Maybe it was caused by some package overriding `print()` (providing that ability was one explicit goal for python to change from the `print` statement to the `print()` function). Too bad I did not take notes.

So I simply tried to be on the careful side here. If someone uses `print()` in the tests for debugging, then I wanted to make sure the same python 3 compatible `print()` is used.

However, that motivation may not be valid enough, and I don't have a strong opinion on that. Let me know if you think that the `print_function` import should be removed again.

Hey Kalani, I think the issue is that "alg" should be a string and not the agent. The following snippet works for me (you'll have to replace the lambda to return an instance of your env and not CartPole of course). 

```
import gym
import ray
from ray.rllib.ppo import ppo
from ray.tune.trial import Trial
from ray.tune.trial_runner import TrialRunner

env = lambda: gym.make("CartPole-v0")
env.env_name = "my_env"   # optionally give your custom env a name
config = ppo.DEFAULT_CONFIG
alg = "PPO"


runner = TrialRunner()
runner.add_trial(Trial(env, alg, config))

ray.init()

while not runner.is_finished():
    runner.step()
    print(runner.debug_string())
```

If `@@` is not considered unique enough syntax that we can make the safe assumption that the user would not need to use it elsewhere, how about `@:/@` instead?

I choose this new syntax for 3 reasons
* `:` cannot be used as part of a file name in Windows
* `/` cannot be used as part of a file name in Linux/Mac
* `:/` describes the emotion of the user when forced to use such awkward syntax, so it's easy to remember.

I'm also open to any alternative solution to solve the problem `@@` was introduced to solve.

If you want to replace the rtmpdump binary, then you have to do it yourself. IMO, it is not worth upgrading it, since more and more platforms are moving to proper streaming protocols, as already mentioned here. We have also talked about it many times now that replacing or pushing new binary files to the Streamlink repo is a bad thing, because it is unnecessarily bloating it up. We first have to rewrite the installer/packaging build steps, so that all binaries can get retrieved from an external place/repo. I don't think that it's worth spending any time on that just for a dependency that's rarely used, but is still working fine in most cases where it is needed.

And if one desperately needs another version, they can still replace it themselves as a user quite easily. In any other case, feel free to contribute the necessary changes here with proper CI build steps for compiling the (patched) rtmpdump executable.

It has been possible to filter by fields on parent datasets for a while now, using `source_filter`.

An example request for Landsat, filtered by GQA would be:
```python
    dc.load(product='ls8_nbar_albers', x=(100.1, 100.2), y=(-35.1, -35.2), 
        source_filter={
            product='ls8_level1_scene',
            gqa_mean_xy: [-1, 1]})
```

When the index is being queried for datasets, this will traverse the tree of parent datasets, until one of the appropriate `product` is found, and then apply the filter.

Re-reading it, perhaps "Check potential allocation of destination array..." is a better phrasing. It can be a function call then - in the single machine case it could be allocated, in a distributed array scenario it could run a function call for all the nodes to see if they are able to hold their portion (either allocating or not as the case maybe), for the "data cube figure it out version" a function again would allow the ODC execution to figure out a way to do it (combo of disk and ram, dask array style).

Looks to me like an "allocation check function before load function" would give the necessary hook point for different deployment methods.

I came up with "task scope" independently from reading the opening post.  I think it's close to ideal.  ("Execution scope" is arguably more accurate, but also more letters.)

When you consider practical usage, it's a clear win over "nursery".  In practice you'd like a short name for accessing the nursery, but `n` is not prudent.  Proposed idiom for task scope using `ts`:
```
async with trio.open_task_scope() as ts:
    ts.run_soon(...)
```

Based on each individual line's content. The actual input is a configuration file for a network switch (ExtremeXOS config is stateless, so no configuration modes like e.g. Cisco has; each line is one independent command). Most of the lines are some variation on "configure [this] [that way]", but I only want two or three [this]es.

A somewhat random sample of lines:

    create vlan "FACTORY"
    configure vlan FACTORY tag 1000
    disable flow-control rx-pause port 1
    configure vlan FACTORY add ports 1-15, 17, 32
    configure iproute add default 172.16.1.1
    disable port 16

Of these lines, I don't want the third and fifth, but I need the others. As I mentioned far above, I could pre-filter, i.e. feed only the lines I know I want to the parser, but once I have analyzed each line thorougly enough to *know* whether I want it, I have already extracted the necessary information as well. The other way around, excluding the lines I don't want, would be the same if done outside the parser, but in the parser I thought I could use negative lookahead.

As far as useful features for parsers go, I would *much* prefer having a clean way to say "when you reach a point where the input fits no known rule, don't panic, but skip ahead past the next newline and try again from there". I suppose this is much more difficult to do than it sounds, however.
